answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Parinde Ki Faryad" ("A Bird's Lament" or "The Bird's Complaint") appears as Chapter 34 in *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (1924).**[^1] It is a poignant Urdu nazm (poem) where a caged bird laments its lost freedom, recalling the garden's beauties, springtime joys, and the pain of captivity—a metaphor for spiritual and existential confinement.

Here is the full English translation:

> [p-f1e47c] A Bird's Lament  
> 
> [p-c41448] Those garden splendors, the chorus of all,  
> The freedom to come and to go at my call.  
> 
> [p-cb3992] My heart aches with pain when I chance to recall,  
> The smiling of buds on the dewdrops that fall.  
> 
> [p-838adc] That beautiful image, that form full of grace,  
> Whose presence brought life to my own dwelling place.  
> 
> [p-9a4316] Her voice never reaches me, held in this plight,  
> Oh, if only release were within my own might!  
> 
> [p-941bb0] How wretched I am, for my home I now pine,  
> My friends in our homeland, this prison is mine.  
> 
> [p-6fadf2] The spring has arrived, and the flowers all bloom,  
> While I weep for my fate in this house of dark gloom.  
> 
> [p-f247dd] O God! To whom can I tell my tale of despair?  
> I fear in this cage I will die from my care.  
> 
> [p-51fe7d] My heart is consuming its grief, it is true,  
> And grief is consuming my heart through and through.  
> 
> [p-7d35b0] O listener, don't think this a song and be glad,  
> It is the cry of all hearts that are wounded and sad.  
> 
> [p-983c20] O Captor, I beg you, now make me unbound,  
> I'm voiceless; release me, a blessing you've found.[^1]

The Urdu original, transliteration, and further context are also available in the corpus.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [34. Parinde Ki Faryad](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/34-parinde-ki-faryad/translation/).
